perturbation.original_text: 1.26 "Net Sales" means, with respect to each Product sold in the Territory, the aggregate gross sales amount invoiced by ETON or any sublicensee or other party authorized by ETON to wholesale or distribute the Products on an arms-length basis to Third Parties in the Territory ("Gross Sales"), less (as applicable) the following ETON expenses as accrued and adjusted for amounts actually taken, consistent with ETON'S standard accounting practices in accordance with GAAP: (a) amounts refunded or credited for returned, damaged, outdated, short-dated or defective goods, and bad debts, and (b) all of the following: (i) taxes, duties and other governmental charges related to the production, use or sale of the Products (including, including without limitation the brand manufacturer's tax imposed pursuant to the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (Pub. L. No. 111-148) as amended or replaced, but not including taxes assessed against the income derived from such sale); (ii) trade, quantity and cash discounts, allowances, retroactive price adjustments, credit incentive payments, chargebacks, patient support programs, and rebates (including governmental rebates or other price reductions provided, based on sales by ETON to any Governmental Entity or regulatory authority in respect of state or federal Medicare, Medicaid, government pricing or similar programs;); and (iii) any costs incurred in connection with or arising out of compliance with any Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies approved by the FDA and (iv) any expenses associated with serialization of the Products. Distribution of Licensed Products for clinical trials or as samples will not be deemed a "Net Sale" under this definition.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.26 "Net Sales" means, with respect to each Product sold in the Territory, the aggregate gross sales amount invoiced by ETON or any sublicensee or other party authorized by ETON to wholesale or distribute the Products on an arms-length basis to Third Parties in the Territory ("Gross Sales"), less (as applicable) amounts refunded or credited for returned, damaged, outdated, short-dated or defective goods, and bad debts. Distribution of Licensed Products for clinical trials or as samples will not be deemed a "Net Sale" under this definition.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the detailed expense deductions from the definition of "Net Sales" creates an in-text contradiction. While the term is still defined, the specific methods for calculating net sales (deducting taxes, discounts, rebates, and compliance costs) have been omitted. This creates uncertainty because sections that reference "Net Sales", such as 6.2 (Commercial Milestones) and 6.3 (Royalty), rely on a clear definition of what constitutes "Net Sales." Without the specifics, there is no clear way to calculate net sales, leading to contradictions in the contract, as the sections still require calculations of net sales. This creates ambiguity in determining the basis for milestone and royalty payments.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2 Commercial Milestones. ETON shall pay to Aucta a total sum amount of up to eighteen million dollars ($18,000,000) based on Net Sales of a Product (on a Product by Product basis) after the achievement of the following one-time milestones for each Product: (a) An amount of one million dollars ($1,000,000) upon Net Sales first exceeding an amount of ten million dollars ($10,000,000) in a calendar year to be paid within sixty (60) days after the calendar year end. (b) An amount of two million dollars ($2,000,000) upon Net Sales first exceeding an amount of twenty million dollars ($20,000,000) in a calendar year to be paid within sixty (60) days after the calendar year end. (c) An amount of five million dollars ($5,000,000) upon Net Sales first exceeding an amount of fifty million dollars ($50,000,000) in a calendar year to be paid within sixty (60) days after the calendar year end. (d) An amount of ten million dollars ($10,000,000) upon Net Sales first exceeding an amount of one hundred million dollars ($100,000,000) in a calendar year to be paid within sixty (60) days after the calendar year end.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a detailed definition of "Net Sales," outlining specific deductions from gross sales, including taxes, discounts, rebates, and compliance costs. The changed text omits these deductions. Section 6.2 explicitly relies on the "Net Sales" figure to determine milestone payments. Since the calculation method for "Net Sales" is now ambiguous due to the omission in the changed text, it creates a direct conflict and uncertainty on how to calculate milestone payments, making section 6.2 unenforceable as written.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4 Medical Inquires, Product Complaints and Recalls. ETON, Aucta and a designated third-party contract manufacturer shall share in the responsibility for responding to any medical inquiries or complaints about any Products or addressing any circumstances that may result in a potential recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval, or similar action ("Recall Event") as set forth in the Pharmacovigilance Agreement attached hereto as Exhibit B (the "Pharmacovigilance Agreement") and to be entered into by the Parties and the contract manufacturer as soon as practicable.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4 Medical Inquires, Product Complaints and Recalls. ETON, Aucta and a designated third-party contract manufacturer shall share in the responsibility for responding to any medical inquiries or complaints about any Products or addressing any circumstances that may result in a potential recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval, or similar action ("Recall Event").
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to the Pharmacovigilance Agreement, Section 3.4 becomes internally contradictory because there are no guidelines on how to share the responsibility for responding to medical inquiries or handling recalls. Without the agreement attached as Exhibit B, there is no way to define the roles and responsibilities of each party, and how Recall Events should be addressed. This affects enforceability, because Section 3.4 still dictates the parties shall 'share in the responsibility', creating ambiguity and potential conflict, therefore it is an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Medical Inquires, Product Complaints and Recalls. ETON, Aucta and a designated third-party contract manufacturer shall share in the responsibility for responding to any medical inquiries or complaints about any Products or addressing any circumstances that may result in a potential recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval, or similar action ("Recall Event").
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the reference to the Pharmacovigilance Agreement, which is intended to define the responsibilities of each party (ETON, Aucta, and the third-party manufacturer) regarding medical inquiries, product complaints, and recalls. By removing this reference, Section 3.4 becomes ambiguous and internally contradictory, as it dictates that the parties 'share in the responsibility' without providing any guidelines or specifics on how this responsibility is to be shared. This directly conflicts with the intention of the original text and creates uncertainty, thus making the agreement difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: 6.7 Audits. Each Party shall permit an independent certified public accounting firm selected by the auditing Party and reasonably acceptable to the non-auditing Party, that has agreed to be bound by a confidentiality agreement reasonably acceptable to the Parties, to have access, during normal business hours and upon reasonable prior notice (not more often than once in any calendar year), to those books and records maintained by the non-auditing Party necessary for the auditing Party to verify the accuracy of the non-auditing Party's calculations under this Section 6 and/or cost of Product(s) for any period ending not more than two (2) years prior to the date of such request, subject to any limitations in scope necessary to comply with Applicable Law, Third Party confidentiality restrictions, or maintain legal privilege, including but not limited to Third Party pricing information. All such information shall be retained on a confidential basis by the accounting firm, and such accounting firm's use of such information shall be limited to the aforementioned verification. Unless otherwise agreed to by the Parties in writing, the accounting firm shall not be paid on a contingency or similar basis.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.7 Audits. Each Party shall permit an accounting professional selected by the auditing Party to have access, during business hours and upon notice, to those books and records maintained by the non-auditing Party necessary for the auditing Party to verify the accuracy of the non-auditing Party's calculations under this Section 6 and/or cost of Product(s).
perturbation.explanation: The removal of specific conditions in Section 6.7 related to who can conduct audits (independent certified public accounting firm, bound by confidentiality), the frequency of audits (not more than once a year), the time frame for which records can be audited (period ending not more than two years prior), and limitations on the scope of access (subject to Applicable Law, etc.), creates an in-text contradiction. The section still allows for audits, but the lack of detail creates ambiguity regarding the scope, frequency, and conduct of these audits. Specifically removing the criteria for a certified independent party and the condition of being bound by a confidentiality agreement is a problem because the section grants access to internal financial documents. Because those factors are not present anymore, it is an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.7 Audits. Each Party shall permit an accounting professional selected by the auditing Party to have access, during business hours and upon notice, to those books and records maintained by the non-auditing Party necessary for the auditing Party to verify the accuracy of the non-auditing Party's calculations under this Section 6 and/or cost of Product(s).
perturbation.justification: The original text of Section 6.7 outlined specific requirements for audits, including the auditor being an independent certified public accounting firm bound by a confidentiality agreement, limitations on the audit's frequency and scope, and the time frame for which records could be accessed. The changed text removes these stipulations, resulting in a contradiction with the original intent and potentially creating a situation where audits are conducted by unqualified individuals, lack proper confidentiality protections, occur too frequently, or access records beyond the intended timeframe. This directly impacts the enforceability and clarity of the audit process, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
